g.data-narration: Welcome to Exception Handling Fundamentals in Java. Learn to handle runtime errors elegantly.
Exception handling prevents program crashes by catching runtime errors and executing recovery code.
g.text: A mechanism to handle runtime errors gracefully, preventing program crashes.
Instead of abrupt termination, exceptions allow controlled error recovery.

g.data-narration: All exceptions derive from java.lang.Throwable, which has Error and Exception as main branches.
Checked exceptions are compiler-enforced; unchecked exceptions occur at runtime and include programming errors.
The try block contains risky code; catch blocks handle exceptions when they occur.
Multiple catch blocks handle different exception types, from specific to general exceptions.
g.text: Handle different exception types - most specific first!

g.data-narration: Nested try statements handle exceptions at multiple levels, with inner catches handling specific errors first.
g.text: Exceptions can be handled at multiple levels

g.data-narration: The throw keyword explicitly throws an exception, creating custom error conditions in your code.
The throws keyword declares exceptions a method might throw, delegating handling to the caller.
The finally block always executes for cleanup, regardless of whether exceptions occur or are caught.
Create custom exceptions by extending Exception class for domain-specific error handling.
Chained exceptions associate one exception with another, preserving the original cause of an error.
This comparison summarizes key differences between checked and unchecked exceptions for exam preparation.
Master these five keywords: try, catch, throw, throws, finally. Practice exception handling with code examples.
For exams: remember order of catch blocks matters, finally always executes, and checked vs unchecked differences.
Practice writing exception handling code to master these concepts for your exams and real-world programming.
